flashcards.back: Memory-mapped I/O is a technique where device registers are mapped to specific memory addresses, allowing the CPU to access I/O devices using standard memory read/write instructions instead of special I/O instructions.

flashcards.front: What is polling (busy-waiting) in I/O operations?
flashcards.back: Polling is an I/O technique where the CPU repeatedly checks a device's status register to determine if it's ready for data transfer. This approach wastes CPU cycles but is simple to implement.

flashcards.front: What are the main components of a typical device interface?
flashcards.back: A typical device interface includes: 1) Status Register (monitors device state), 2) Data Register (transfers data), and 3) Control Register (sends commands to the device).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between memory-mapped I/O and port-mapped I/O?
flashcards.back: Memory-mapped I/O uses memory addresses to access devices, while port-mapped I/O uses special I/O instructions and separate I/O address space. Memory-mapped I/O doesn't require special instructions but consumes memory address space.

flashcards.front: What are the alternatives to polling for I/O operations?
flashcards.back: Alternatives to polling include: 1) Interrupt-driven I/O (device interrupts CPU when ready), and 2) DMA (Direct Memory Access) where a separate controller handles data transfer without CPU involvement.
